Project Foundations for Data Science: FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Marks: 40

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. Online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app.

The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirms the pick-up in the app and travels to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in their online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand of different restaurants which will help them in enhancing their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company to improve the business.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

Data Dictionary¶

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

In [ ]:
# import libraries for data manipulation
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# import libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Understanding the structure of the data¶

In [ ]:
# read the data
df = pd.read_csv('foodhub_order.csv')
# returns the first 5 rows
df.head()
Out[ ]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend Not given 25 20
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24

Observations:¶

The DataFrame has 9 columns as mentioned in the Data Dictionary. Data in each row corresponds to the order placed by a customer.

Question 1: How many rows and columns are present in the data?¶

In [ ]:
print("Rows:", df.shape[0], "\nColumns:", df.shape[1])

Rows: 1898 
Columns: 9

Observations:¶

As we can see, the dataset has a total of 1896 rows, with each row having 9 columns

We can see that Order ID and Customer ID are categorical variables, so we may want to change their type from int to object

Question 3: Are there any missing values in the data? If yes, treat them using an appropriate method¶

In [ ]:
df.isna().sum()
Out[ ]:
order_id                 0
customer_id              0
restaurant_name          0
cuisine_type             0
cost_of_the_order        0
day_of_the_week          0
rating                   0
food_preparation_time    0
delivery_time            0
dtype: int64

Observations:¶

We have no missing values in our data

In [ ]:
df["rating"].value_counts()["Not given"]
Out[ ]:
736

If we wish to treat the not given values in rating as missing values, then the amount of missing values in rating would be 736, we can fix this by changing it to a float/integer data type and replace the not given values with 0

In [ ]:
# Replace the not given value to 0
df['rating'] = df['rating'].replace(['Not given'],0)

# ..Then Change the data types accordingly

df = df.astype({"rating": int})


df.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>

Question 4: Check the statistical summary of the data. What is the minimum, average, and maximum time it takes for food to be prepared once an order is placed?¶

In [ ]:
df.describe()
Out[ ]:
cost_of_the_order rating food_preparation_time delivery_time
count 1898.000000 1898.000000 1898.000000 1898.000000
mean 16.498851 2.659642 27.371970 24.161749
std 7.483812 2.195280 4.632481 4.972637
min 4.470000 0.000000 20.000000 15.000000
25% 12.080000 0.000000 23.000000 20.000000

75% 22.297500 5.000000 31.000000 28.000000
max 35.410000 5.000000 35.000000 33.000000

Out of our 1898 observations, the minimum, maximum, and average time taken to prepare food is 20 mins, 35 mins and 27.372 mins repectively

Question 5: How many orders are not rated?¶

We can see that Rating is a column with data in mostly integer form, yet is categorized as object. We can correct this by assigning it the float datatype instead, and replacing the "Not Given"s with -1

In [ ]:
# Write the code here
df['rating'].value_counts()
Out[ ]:
rating
0    736
5    588
4    386
3    188
Name: count, dtype: int64

Observations:¶

Since we replaced all of the values where no rating was given to 0, the amount of orders where no rating was given is 736

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Univariate Analysis¶

Question 6: Explore all the variables and provide observations on their distributions. (Generally, histograms, boxplots, countplots, etc. are used for univariate exploration)¶

Based on Cost of Orders¶

In [ ]:
sns.histplot(data=df, x="cost_of_the_order", bins=15, kde=True)
plt.show()
sns.violinplot(data=df, x="cost_of_the_order", color="cyan")
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data=df, x="cost_of_the_order", color="cyan")
Out[ ]:
<AxesSubplot: xlabel='cost_of_the_order'>

Observation:¶

Our histogram is skewed to the left towards the lower costs with there being a peak around 13-14 dollars

Our boxplot/Violinplot illustrates that the median cost is around 13-14 dollars, with the results being skewed to the right

Based on Cuisine Type¶

In [ ]:
plt.figure(figsize=(20,9))
sns.countplot(data=df, x='cuisine_type')
Out[ ]:
<AxesSubplot: xlabel='cuisine_type', ylabel='count'>

Observation:¶

The most popular cuisines are American, Japanese, Italian and Chinese, followed by Mexican, Indian, Middle Eastern and Mediterranean in that order

Based on Ratings¶

Since all orders rated 0 were originally not rated, we won't be tasking them into consideration in our readings

In [ ]:
sns.countplot(data=df[df["rating"]!=0], x="rating")
plt.show()
sns.histplot(data=df[df["rating"]!=0], x="rating", bins=5, kde=True)
plt.show()
sns.violinplot(data=df[df["rating"]!=0], x="rating", color="cyan")
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data=df[df["rating"]!=0], x="rating", color="cyan")
Out[ ]:
<AxesSubplot: xlabel='rating'>

Observations:¶

On the Histplot and Countplot Ratings of the orders is heavily concentrated towards the higher ratings

On the boxplot/Violinplot, the orders are so heacily skewed to the left that we dont have a right whisker

Based on days of the week¶

In [ ]:
sns.countplot(data=df, x="day_of_the_week")
plt.show()

Observations:¶

More people prefer to order food on weekends over weekdays

Based on Preparation time¶

In [ ]:
sns.histplot(data=df, x="food_preparation_time", bins=16, kde=True)
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data=df, x="food_preparation_time", color="cyan")
Out[ ]:
<AxesSubplot: xlabel='food_preparation_time'>

Observations:¶

Based on the histogram, the preparsation time of food is very even between 20 and 35 minutes

Based on boxplot, the median is sround 27 minutes

Based on delivery time¶

In [ ]:
sns.histplot(data=df, x="delivery_time", bins=10, kde=True)
plt.show()
sns.boxplot(data=df, x="delivery_time", color="cyan")
Out[ ]:
<AxesSubplot: xlabel='delivery_time'>

Observations:¶

From the histplot, we can see most orders are delivered around 24-29 minutes

From boxplot, we observe that the observations are left skewed with the median being around 25 minutes

Question 9: What percentage of the orders cost more than 20 dollars?¶

total = df.cost_of_the_order.count()
orders_above_20 = df.cost_of_the_order[df["cost_of_the_order"]>20].count()

print((orders_above_20/total)*100)

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer¶

In [ ]:
dfmean = df[["restaurant_name","rating"]][df["rating"]!= 0].groupby('restaurant_name').mean()>4
dfcount = df[["restaurant_name","rating"]][df["rating"]!= 0].groupby('restaurant_name').count()>50
pd.merge(dfmean[dfmean['rating']==True], dfcount[dfcount['rating']==True], on="restaurant_name")
Out[ ]:
rating_x rating_y
restaurant_name
Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken True True
Blue Ribbon Sushi True True
Shake Shack True True
The Meatball Shop True True

Observations:¶

Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken, Blue Ribbon Sushi, Shake Shack, and The Meatball Shop qualify for the promotional offer

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders¶

Variables used:¶

rev - is total revenue generated

cost - is a pandas series of cost of orders from our data

x - is the income generated from each order systematically

In [ ]:
#rev and x are initialized to zero before our loop begins
x = 0
rev = 0

#cost is assigned the cost_of_the_order column from our dataframe as a series
cost = pd.Series(df["cost_of_the_order"])
In [ ]:
#Running loop using cost to calculate revenue generated

for i in range(len(cost)):
    if cost[i]>20:
        income = cost[i] * 0.25
    elif (cost[i]>5) & (cost[i]<20):
        income = cost[i] * 0.15
    else:
        income = 0
    
    rev = rev + income
print("$", round(rev, 2), sep="")

$6166.3

Observations:¶

Revenue generated by FoodHub is $6166.30 based on the data given

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered)¶

In [ ]:
# Percent of orders above 60mins = (No. of Orders with delivery time over 60mins / Total Orders) * 100
percent60above = (df[df["food_preparation_time"] + df["delivery_time"]>60]["order_id"].count() / df["order_id"].count()) * 100

print(round(percent60above,2), "%" , sep="")

10.54%

Observations:¶

About 10.54% of orders take more than 60 minutes to reach the customer from the time the order was placed

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends?¶

In [ ]:
df.groupby("day_of_the_week")[["delivery_time"]].mean()
Out[ ]:
delivery_time
day_of_the_week
Weekday 28.340037
Weekend 22.470022

Observations:¶

We can see that average delivery time during the weekends is 22.47mins compared to 28.34mins during the weekdays

This can be due to higher traffic during working hours on weekdays compared to weekends

Conclusion and Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations)¶

Conclusions:¶

  • The popularity of the cuisines remains consistent over the week, irrespective of weekday or weekends
  • There is a higher frequency of orders over the weekends compared to the weekdays
  • The cost of orders peak at around 13-14 dollars
  • Food preparation time is fairly consistent, whereas delivery time is comparitively irregular
  • Most of the ratings are skewd towards the 4-5 star range, excluding the amount of orders left unrated(around 39%), leaving a possibility of an error in the true rating value of the respective restaurants
  • We can see that customers getting their orders quicker also tend to leave the reviews higher on average

Recommendations:¶

  • Given that the cuisines show consistent popularity throughout the week, focus can be put on boosting the amount of orders during the weekdays

  • The higher frequency of orders during weekends compared to weekdays could be due to individuals not preferring to order food to their workplace, but rather go out and eat at the establishments themselves so as to save time, given the fact that delivery times have been observed to be higher on weekdays. There can be a focus on improving the delivery times by providing better navigational features, by leaving a review section for the customers such that they can suggest paths for the order to reach them faster and reduce delivery time can be made. This will also lead to better ratings by the customers, since lower delivery times have been seen to lead to higher rating scores

  • Given that the cost of orders is heavily skewed towards the lower price ranges, with it peaking around 14 dollars, it can be assumed that people with lower/limited income(like Students) or day to day office workers may be the larger part of the demographic

  • Customers should be given incentives to leave reviews and ratings on their orders, like coupon deals per a given amount of reviews, to reduce the not given ratings on orders. Though this may lead to some customers leaving false ratings just for the offers, it should have a largely positive impact on the overall data collected for future use

  • Extending the last point, customers should be encouraged to leave a written review along their numbered rating such that false ratings can be picked apart from the genuine ones for cleaner data
